title: Chapter 38: Infiltration
content: The maintenance shaft gaped open like a throat.

Junko had pryed the access panel off the HVAC unit with a wrench she'd produced from somewhere inside her jacket, and now the vertical passage yawned before them — a rectangle of absolute darkness barely wide enough for a human body, vanishing upward into the guts of Vale Industries Tower. The air that breathed up from below smelled of machine oil, copper wiring, and the particular stale nothing of spaces where no one had breathed in weeks.

Junko clicked on a penlight and tossed it into the shaft. The tiny beam tumbled end over end, illuminating segments of the climb in strobed flashes — ductwork, pipe junctions, horizontal rungs set into the concrete at irregular intervals. The light fell for four seconds before clattering to a stop on a landing thirty feet below.
Wrong direction. The shaft went down before it went up.

"The HVAC plant is on thirty-five," Junko said, reading his expression. "Which means we drop to the main trunk line first, then climb the auxiliary stack to the upper floors. It's a detour, but the auxiliary is narrower and unmonitored. Crane's people don't know it exists."
"How do you know it exists?" Elena asked.

"I was contracted to break into this building three years ago." Junko's voice was flat. "A competitor wanted the floor plans. I memorized them instead of delivering. Even then, some habits were stronger than loyalty."
Marcus filed that away — another fragment of Junko's past, another debt she was paying in candor. He looked at Sera.

She was staring into the shaft with an expression he couldn't parse. Not fear. Not hesitation. Something older, deeper — the look of someone approaching a house they'd once owned, knowing the furniture would be rearranged, the walls repainted, the ownership transferred to strangers who didn't know what the place had meant.
"I'll go first," she said. Her voice was steady but quiet. "I know this building."
She dropped into the darkness before anyone could argue.

The descent was twelve feet of vertical concrete, then a horizontal crawl through ductwork that left Marcus's shoulders scraping both walls and his knees bruising against junction seams. Behind him, Elena's breath came in controlled measures — three counts in, three counts out, a nurse's rhythm. Behind her, Junko moved in near-silence, the only noise the faint whisper of her jacket against metal.

Sera was already ahead, somewhere in the dark, her Shadow Weave providing just enough ambient glow to mark the turns. Left at the first junction. Right at the second. Through a narrower passage where the ductwork compressed until they were commando-crawling on elbows and forearms, the claustrophobia pressing down like a physical weight.
Then the passage opened, and they emerged into the HVAC plant.

It was a cathedral of machinery — massive air handling units standing in rows like monoliths, ductwork branching from them in arteries of sheet metal, catwalks and service ladders running between them in a maze that smelled of dust and refrigerant. Emergency lighting — powered by whatever backup generator Crane had activated — cast everything in a dull amber glow.
Floor thirty-five. Sera's floor. The executive level.

"The plant's clear," Junko said, appearing from behind an air handler with her penlight clipped to her collar. She'd scouted while they were still crawling. "No guards. Crane pulled his people up to thirty-six when he fortified the stairwells. He's bottlenecking, not distributing."
"Which means the stairwell between thirty-five and thirty-six will be guarded," Marcus said.

"Heavily." Junko held up two fingers, then four, then made a fist. "At least six on the landing. Barricade at the door. Full combat load from the look of it — rifles, body armor, one of them had a belt-fed." She paused. "We can't take that head-on."

"Then we don't." Sera's voice came from the far end of the plant. She was standing at a door — a regular door, steel-framed, unmarked except for a small plaque that read EXECUTIVE WING. Her hand rested on the handle but didn't push it. "The executive wing has a separate stairwell. Private access. It was installed for the CEO — for me — after the 2019 security audit. It connects all floors from thirty-three to forty without touching the main stairwells."
Junko's eyebrows rose. "A private staircase?"
"The board insisted." A breath. "They were worried about activist infiltration."

She still hadn't opened the door. Marcus crossed the distance between them, stepping over coolant lines and conduit housings, until he was standing at her shoulder. Through the Bond, he felt it — the turbulence beneath her calm surface, the undertow of memory pulling at her ankles.
"Sera."

"My office is through there." She nodded at the door. "Corner suite. Northeast facing. I picked it myself when we moved headquarters in 2021. I chose the view — the harbor, the bridges, the mountains on clear days." Her jaw tightened. "I used to sit at that desk and think I'd built something real. Something that would outlast me."
"Hey." He touched her arm. Her skin was cool through the Shadow Weave. "You *did* build something real. The building's still standing. Your sister's still alive in it. That counts."

"Does it?" Her silver eyes met his, and in them he saw something she rarely showed — not the CEO's composure, not the zombie queen's ferocity, but the raw grief of a woman who'd woken up dead and discovered that the empire she'd sacrificed everything for had become a warlord's fortress. "I spent fourteen-hour days in that office. I missed Lily's piano recitals. I forgot my mother's birthday three years running. I told myself the company was the priority and the family would understand, and then the world ended, and now the company is Crane's and my sister is suffocating in a vault I designed to protect documents, not people."
The words fell like stones into water. Elena and Junko hung back, giving her space — understanding, perhaps, that some moments weren't meant for audiences.
Marcus didn't give her space. He stepped closer, cupped her face in both hands, and held her gaze.

"You built a vault that kept your sister alive for twenty-three days in the apocalypse." His voice was low and fierce. "You built a company that was worth fortifying, worth fighting for, worth a warlord claiming as his crown jewel. And you survived long enough to come back and get her." He pressed his forehead to hers. "That's not failure, Sera. That's the longest game anyone's ever played for their sister's life."

She closed her eyes. A single tear — cold, silver-tinged, the metabolic byproduct of a body that wasn't quite alive and wasn't quite dead — traced down her cheek. She leaned into him for exactly three seconds, drawing something from the contact that Marcus couldn't name but felt through the Bond like oxygen entering a drowning person's lungs.
Then she straightened. Wiped her cheek. Opened the door.
The executive wing of Vale Industries was a ghost ship.

The carpet was plush, industrial, the kind designed to absorb sound in high-traffic corridors — and it absorbed their footsteps now with the same efficiency, turning four people into whispers. The walls were hung with abstract art that Sera had personally selected, each piece chosen to project a specific message about innovation and confidence and whatever brand identity the quarterly report demanded. The lighting was emergency-only, strips along the baseboard that painted everything in a thin, bloodless line of white.

Doors lined the corridor. VP of Operations. VP of Finance. General Counsel. Each nameplate was a small tombstone marking a job that no longer existed, a person who was dead or turned or scattered across the ruins of the old world.
Sera walked past them without looking.
At the end of the corridor, the corner office. Double doors, frosted glass, the name stenciled in clean corporate lettering:

**SERAPHINA VALE**
**CHIEF EXECUTIVE OFFICER**
She stopped. Her hand rose toward the handle — hovered — then fell to her side.
"Later," she said, and kept walking.

Marcus didn't push. Through the Bond, he felt the wound she'd just自行 cauterized — the grief packed down, compartmentalized, fuel for the engine of survival. It wasn't healing. It wasn't resolution. It was a woman putting her own heart in a vault because there wasn't time to feel it, and the vault had a timer just like Lily's, and eventually it would run out of air too.

The private stairwell was behind a panel in the far wall — invisible unless you knew to press the third decorative sconce from the left. The panel slid open with a soft pneumatic hiss, revealing a narrow concrete staircase spiraling upward.

Junko went first. She moved like smoke, each step placed with the precision of a woman who could kill in three inches of space, her body compact and coiled and ready. Sera followed, then Marcus, then Elena bringing up the rear with her medical bag clutched to her chest like a talisman.

Floor thirty-six. Junko held up a fist. Through the stairwell door — inches of reinforced steel — came the muffled sounds of occupation: voices, boot heels on tile, the clink of equipment. The guards were on the other side. Six of them, according to Junko's count. Maybe more now, if Crane had reinforced since the flare.
They climbed.

Floor thirty-seven. The sounds were louder here — raider activity on the other side of the stairwell door, the guttural cadence of men who'd claimed territory and were comfortable in it. Someone laughed. Someone made a joke. The laughter was the worst part — easy, casual, the laughter of people who'd stopped noticing that the world was dead.

Floor thirty-eight. A checkpoint. Junko pressed her ear to the door and held up four fingers, then pointed at the door and made a rotating motion. Four guards, rotating patrol. She pointed at the ceiling, then traced a line toward the upper floors. The vent system.
Marcus understood. The vents.

Junko had mentioned it before — the infrastructure of Vale Industries was a second city layered inside the first, ducts and shafts and maintenance passages that ran between every floor like capillaries in a body. For normal humans, the vents were impassable — too narrow, too hot, too disorienting. But for a Stage 2 zombie with enhanced flexibility and zero body fat?
Junko pointed at herself, then at the ceiling, then up. Two floors. Through the vents. She'd come out on forty.
Marcus looked at Sera. Through the Bond, her assent flowed — logical, tactical, trusting Junko's assessment of her own capabilities even if she didn't yet trust Junko herself.
He nodded.

Junko tilted her head toward the ceiling access panel — a square grate near the top of the stairwell, twelve feet up, barely visible in the emergency lighting. She jumped. Not a running jump, not a climbing jump — a *launch*, her legs uncoiling with Stage 2 force, her body rising like she'd been shot from a cannon in compression. Her fingers caught the grate, peeled it open, and she was inside, swallowed by the ceiling in the space between one heartbeat and the next.
The grate settled back into place. Silence.
Sera and Marcus exchanged a look.
"Now we wait," Sera murmured.

She leaned against the stairwell wall, and her gaze drifted downward — back toward thirty-five, back toward the office with her name on the door, back toward the ghost of the woman she'd been before the infection and the Bond and the long road through the apocalypse that had brought her home to a building she no longer owned.
The System pulsed in Marcus's vision:
**[Lily Vale — Air Supply: 34 hours, 11 minutes remaining]**
**[Current Position: Floor 38, Private Stairwell]**
**[Junko Tanaka — Vent Infiltration IN PROGRESS]**
**[Estimated time to breach Floor 40: 8 minutes]**
**[Hostile Forces on Floor 40: 6 armed combatants]**
**[Junko's Assessment: 'I can handle six.']**
**[THE CLOCK IS TICKING.]**
Eight minutes. Six guards. One sister running out of air.
Marcus pressed his shoulder against the wall beside Sera and waited for the signal that would change everything.

Above them, in the darkness between floors, Junko Tanaka crawled through the veins of a building she'd once been paid to infiltrate, moving toward a vault that held a woman she'd never met, carrying the hope of a sister who was no longer just a mission parameter but a person — real, fragile, running out of time.
The vent shaft was hot and tight and reminded her of a coffin she'd once hidden in for fourteen hours during a hit in Osaka. She'd been twenty-two. Her father had said *well done, Junko*.
She crawled faster.

Floor thirty-nine. The vent passed over a room where she could hear voices — low, tense, the timbre of men who knew something was coming. Crane's inner circle, maybe. Or just soldiers smart enough to be afraid.

Floor forty. Through the grille, she could see the corridor. barricades constructed from desks and filing cabinets. The vault door at the far end — massive, circular, the kind of engineering that said *nothing gets through*. Two guards at the barricade. Two more at the vault. Two patrolling the corridor.
Six.

Junko flexed her fingers. The old familiar electricity ran through her forearms — muscle memory encoded at the cellular level, the muscle memory of a woman who'd broken people for a living and broken herself in the process.
*For Lily*, she thought. *For the sister of the zombie who saw me eat on a curb and didn't look away.*
She found the vent grille's release catches. Twisted them, slow and silent.
And dropped into hell.
